title: Music Theory (Notation, Scales, Intervals, Chords, Key & Time Signatures)

competence_markers.description: The student can identify common intervals by ear with at least 80% accuracy (unison through octave)
The student can play and sing major and natural minor scales in at least six keys using solfege
The student can identify the four triad types by ear (major, minor, diminished, augmented)
The student can describe the harmonic function of I, IV, and V chords and hear tonic-dominant-tonic motion in music
The student can play the 12-bar blues progression in at least two keys
The student can sight-sing a simple melody using solfege from notation
The student knows the circle of fifths and can identify key signatures
The student can read standard notation on the treble and bass clef with increasing fluency

narration_prompts.prompt: Play me two intervals. Name them and explain how you can tell them apart by ear. What song or mnemonic helps you remember each one?
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **Play a major scale and a minor scale starting on the same note. Describe what is different — both in the pattern of steps and in how they sound. Which feels brighter? Which feels darker?**

narration_prompts.prompt: Play a I-IV-V-I progression. Explain what each chord is doing: where is "home"? Where is the tension? Where is the resolution? Why does this progression work?
narration_prompts.detail: 4. **What is the 12-bar blues? Play it and explain why this single chord progression matters so much to American music history.**

narration_prompts.prompt: Sing a simple melody from notation using solfege. Then explain what solfege does for your ear — why is naming the scale degrees useful?
narration_prompts.detail: 6. **Explain the circle of fifths. Why is it the map of Western harmony? How does it help you understand key signatures?**

cross_connections.description: Time signatures and rhythmic notation studied there are the temporal framework for the pitch notation studied here. Rhythm and pitch together constitute the full language of music notation.
The theoretical vocabulary built here (intervals, chords, keys, progressions) is the toolkit for the formal and structural analysis in B2. Theory and listening must develop in parallel.
Scales, arpeggios, sight-reading, and harmonic understanding are directly applied in instrument study and vocal technique.

Intervals are frequency ratios. The octave is 2:1, the fifth is 3:2, the fourth is 4:3. Scales are number patterns. The mathematics of music is real and illuminating for students who are developing algebraic thinking.

Musical systems developed within religious and cultural contexts. Gregorian chant, the Islamic adhan, Hindu devotional music, and Buddhist chanting each use specific scales, modes, and melodic principles tied to their theological and cultural frameworks.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been hearing music for years. You can recognize instruments, sing melodies, and describe what you hear. Now it is time to understand the system — the grammar and vocabulary of how music is built.

Music theory is not a set of rules imposed on music from outside. It is a description of what trained ears have always heard. Scales describe the sets of notes that melodies use. Intervals describe the distances between notes. Chords describe what happens when notes sound together. Key signatures tell you which set of notes a piece is built from. Time signatures tell you how the beats are organized. None of this was invented to make music harder. All of it was developed to describe what was already happening — to give musicians a shared language for talking about, writing down, and building upon the patterns that make music work.

Theory without listening is empty. You must hear what you study. Every scale, interval, and chord in this packet should be played, sung, and heard — not just read about on paper. The goal is not to pass a theory test. The goal is to hear music with understanding: to know, when a chord progression moves from V to I, not just what that means in a textbook but what it sounds like and feels like and why every pop song in history uses it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Interval recognition by ear** — hearing the distance between two notes and identifying it
2. **Scale comprehension** — understanding major, minor, pentatonic, and modal scales as systems, not just sequences of notes
3. **Harmonic understanding** — knowing how chords are built, what they sound like, and how they function in progressions
4. **Notation fluency** — reading treble and bass clef, key signatures, and standard notation with increasing speed
5. **Solfege or equivalent pitch system** — a method for naming scale degrees that trains the ear to hear tonal relationships
6. **Sight-singing** — the ability to read a simple melody from notation and sing it accurately
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Notation — The Written Language of Music

Musical notation is a system for writing down the two essential elements of music: **pitch** (how high or low a note is) and **duration** (how long it lasts).

**The staff** is five horizontal lines. Notes are placed on the lines and in the spaces between them. Higher notes are placed higher on the staff; lower notes are placed lower. The **treble clef** (also called the G clef) marks the staff used for higher instruments and voices. The **bass clef** (also called the F clef) marks the staff used for lower instruments and voices.

In the treble clef, the lines from bottom to top are E-G-B-D-F. The spaces spell F-A-C-E. In the bass clef, the lines are G-B-D-F-A and the spaces are A-C-E-G. Memorize these. They are the alphabet of musical notation.

**Note values** (duration) were covered in 3.4.1-B: whole notes, half notes, quarter notes, eighth notes, sixteenth notes, dotted notes, ties, and rests. If you need review, return to that material.

**Key signatures** appear at the beginning of each line of music, between the clef and the time signature. Sharps or flats in the key signature tell you which notes are raised or lowered throughout the piece. A key signature with one sharp (F#) means the piece is in G major or E minor. A key signature with three flats (Bb, Eb, Ab) means the piece is in Eb major or C minor. You will learn the full circle of fifths, which organizes all the keys in a logical pattern.

### Part 2: Intervals — The Distances Between Notes

An **interval** is the distance between two pitches. Intervals are the building blocks of melody (notes in sequence) and harmony (notes sounding together).

Learn to recognize these intervals by ear:

- **Unison** — the same note. No distance.
- **Minor second** — one half-step. Dissonant, tense, close. Think of the *Jaws* theme.
- **Major second** — one whole step. The first two notes of a major scale ascending.
- **Minor third** — three half-steps. The "sad" interval. First two notes of "Greensleeves."
- **Major third** — four half-steps. The "happy" interval. First two notes of "Oh! When the Saints Go Marching In."
- **Perfect fourth** — five half-steps. Open, strong. "Here Comes the Bride."
- **Tritone** — six half-steps. The most dissonant interval. Unstable, restless. Opening of "The Simpsons" theme.
- **Perfect fifth** — seven half-steps. Stable, open, hollow. "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star."
- **Minor sixth** — eight half-steps. Bittersweet. Opening of "The Entertainer."
- **Major sixth** — nine half-steps. Warm, bright. "My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean."
- **Minor seventh** — ten half-steps. Bluesy tension. Opening of the original *Star Trek* theme.
- **Major seventh** — eleven half-steps. Dreamy, unresolved tension.
- **Octave** — twelve half-steps. The same note, higher. "Somewhere Over the Rainbow."

Interval recognition is ear training at its most fundamental. Practice it regularly — at the piano, with an app, with your mentor calling out intervals for you to identify. Over time, each interval develops its own personality in your ear, as recognizable as a familiar voice.

### Part 3: Scales — The Note Collections of Music

A **scale** is a set of notes arranged in ascending order that provides the raw material for melodies and harmonies in a piece of music. Different scales produce different musical colors.

**The major scale** uses the pattern whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half (W-W-H-W-W-W-H). Starting on C: C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C. This is the most familiar scale in Western music — it sounds bright, stable, and resolved. Every major scale, regardless of starting note, uses this same pattern of whole steps and half steps.

**The natural minor scale** uses the pattern W-H-W-W-H-W-W. Starting on A: A-B-C-D-E-F-G-A. Minor scales sound darker, sadder, or more serious than major scales. The difference between major and minor is one of the most important color distinctions in Western music.

**The pentatonic scale** contains only five notes (penta = five). The major pentatonic removes the fourth and seventh degrees from a major scale. Starting on C: C-D-E-G-A. This scale appears in folk music from virtually every culture on earth. It contains no half-steps and therefore no strong dissonance, which makes it universally accessible. If you improvise using only pentatonic notes, almost everything you play will sound good — which is why it is the first scale taught to beginning improvisers.

**Modes** are scales built from the same notes as a major scale but starting on different degrees. Starting a C major scale on D gives you **Dorian** mode (D-E-F-G-A-B-C-D) — minor in character but with a raised sixth that gives it a melancholy-but-not-tragic quality. "Scarborough Fair" is in Dorian. Starting on E gives **Phrygian** (E-F-G-A-B-C-D-E) — the lowered second degree creates a Spanish or Middle Eastern character. Each mode has its own distinct color, and learning to hear them expands your tonal vocabulary beyond the major-minor binary.

### Part 4: Solfege — A Language for the Ear

**Solfege** (do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do) gives each scale degree a name. In **movable-do** solfege, "do" is always the first note of whatever key you are in. This means the solfege syllables describe the function of each note within the key, not its absolute pitch.

Why does this matter? Because tonal function is what your ear hears. The leading tone (ti) feels like it wants to resolve upward to the tonic (do) in every key. The dominant (sol) feels stable and strong in every key. By naming the scale degrees with solfege, you give your ear a handle for recognizing these relationships regardless of the key.

Practice singing scales in solfege: do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do. Then practice intervals: do to mi (major third), do to sol (perfect fifth), mi to sol (minor third). Then sight-sing simple melodies from notation using solfege. This skill — translating written notes into sounds through the solfege system — is one of the most valuable you will develop.

### Part 5: Chords and Harmony

When two or more notes sound at the same time, they create **harmony**. The most basic harmonic unit is the **triad** — three notes stacked in thirds.

**Major triad** — root, major third, perfect fifth. Sounds bright and stable. (C-E-G)
**Minor triad** — root, minor third, perfect fifth. Sounds dark and stable. (C-Eb-G)
**Diminished triad** — root, minor third, diminished fifth. Sounds tense and unstable. (C-Eb-Gb)
**Augmented triad** — root, major third, augmented fifth. Sounds tense and suspended. (C-E-G#)

Play each of these at the piano. Hear the difference. Major and minor triads are the workhorses of Western harmony. Diminished and augmented triads create tension that demands resolution.

**Chord progressions** describe how chords move from one to the next. The most fundamental progression in Western music is **I-IV-V-I** — tonic (home) to subdominant (departure) to dominant (tension) to tonic (return home). This progression, in various forms, underlies an astonishing proportion of Western music from the Baroque era to today's pop charts.

**The 12-bar blues** is a specific chord progression: I-I-I-I, IV-IV-I-I, V-IV-I-I (with a V turnaround at the end). This single progression generated jazz, rock and roll, R&B, and much of American popular music. Learn to play it in at least two keys. Hear it in recordings. Understand that when someone says American music descended from the blues, they are describing a literal harmonic genealogy.

**Voice leading** — the principle that smooth chord progressions move each note as little as possible to the next chord. Good voice leading creates a sense of inevitability: each chord sounds like the natural next step from the one before. The resolution of ti (the leading tone) up to do (the tonic) is the fundamental harmonic motion that defines Western tonality. When you hear a song end on a V chord without resolving to I, the sense of incompleteness you feel is your ear demanding that voice leading resolve.

### Part 6: The Circle of Fifths

The **circle of fifths** is a diagram that organizes all twelve major and minor keys by their relationship to each other. Moving clockwise, each key is a fifth higher and adds one sharp. Moving counterclockwise, each key is a fifth lower and adds one flat. Adjacent keys on the circle share all but one note, which is why modulations to nearby keys sound smooth.

Memorize the circle of fifths. It is the map of tonal space — the geography of Western harmony. Knowing it means you can instantly identify key signatures, find related keys, and understand why certain modulations feel natural and others feel distant.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Interval Recognition
**Developing well:** The student identifies intervals by ear with 80%+ accuracy. They have personal mnemonics or song associations for each interval. When they hear an interval in music, they can name it.

**Developing slowly:** Accuracy is below 60%. The student confuses thirds with sixths, or fourths with fifths.

**Diagnostic:** More focused practice on the confused intervals. Play only the two intervals the student confuses, back and forth, until the difference becomes clear. Isolation and repetition resolve confusion faster than comprehensive drilling.

### 2. Harmonic Hearing
**Developing well:** The student can hear the difference between major and minor triads reliably. They can follow a simple chord progression (I-IV-V-I) in a recording and identify when each chord changes.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish major from minor triads by ear. Chord progressions sound like undifferentiated accompaniment.

**Diagnostic:** Start with the most extreme contrast: play a major triad, then a minor triad built on the same root. The only note that changes is the third. Repeat this until the student can hear the color difference. Then move to identifying triads in isolation. Chord progression hearing develops after triad recognition is solid.

### 3. Sight-Singing
**Developing well:** The student can sing a simple melody from notation using solfege with reasonable accuracy on the first attempt. Intervals are correct. Rhythm is correct.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot translate notation to sound without playing it on an instrument first.

**Diagnostic:** Is the student practicing sight-singing regularly? This skill requires daily practice with graduated difficulty. Start with melodies that move only by step, then add leaps of thirds, then fifths, then other intervals. Use simple, well-known melodies as starting material.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Theory Without Sound
The most common failure in music theory instruction is treating it as a paper-and-pencil subject. The student who can identify intervals on a worksheet but not by ear has learned the wrong thing. Every written exercise should be preceded or followed by an aural exercise.

### Memorization Without Understanding
The student who memorizes "G major has one sharp, F#" without understanding why (because G major follows the W-W-H-W-W-W-H pattern and F must be raised to maintain it) has memorized a fact without understanding a system. Push for understanding of the system, not just memorization of its products.

### Skipping the Blues
The 12-bar blues is not a footnote. It is the harmonic foundation of the largest body of popular music in the world. The student who understands the blues progression understands American music history in harmonic form. Do not rush past it.
